sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: LEFT.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 19, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: Level 1 has two white chambers separated by a purple vertical bar. The left chamber has a charcoal frame and a green frame with white center. Between chambers is a passage with a green frame (black center) and a white 3x3 square. The right chamber has a charcoal frame. The white 3x3 square at [30-32, 30-32] appears to be the player avatar in the passage area.
- Goal model: Navigate the player through the passage to the right chamber.
- Action model: UP/DOWN/LEFT/RIGHT likely move the white square avatar.
- Plan: Continue moving RIGHT to get the player through the passage.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 18 of about 28 target actions so far.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Now I can see the pattern more clearly. After moving LEFT:
- id=6 (green frame, 8 pixels) is at [30,27]-[32,29] with child=[9] (white dot at [31,28])
- id=7 (white, 9 pixels) is at [30,30]-[32,32] - this is the player back in the passage area
- id=8 (green frame, 8 pixels) is at [30,42]-[32,44] with child=[10] (black dot)
So the player moved from the right chamber back to the passage. The green frame that was in the passage (id=6) moved left into the left chamber area, carrying the white dot. The player (id=7) is now a white 3x3 square at [30,30]-[32,32].
Wait, let me reconsider. The player seems to be the white 3x3 square (id=7, 9 pixels). When it moves into the right chamber, it transforms into a green frame (id=7 becomes green with 8 pixels). When it moves back to the passage, it becomes white again.
But actually, looking more carefully:
- Before LEFT: player was id=7 (green frame, 8 pixels) at [30,42]-[32,44] in right chamber
- After LEFT: id=7 is now white (9 pixels) at [30,30]-[32,32] in passage
So the player is id=7, and it transforms based on location:
- In passage: white 3x3 square (9 pixels)
- In right chamber: green frame (8 pixels) with black dot inside
The passage green frame (id=6) moved left to [30,27]-[32,29] when the player moved left.
And id=8 (green frame) is now at [30,42]-[32,44] in the right chamber - this seems to be the green frame that was left behind when the player moved out.
So the mechanic seems to be:
1. The player (white 3x3 square) moves through the passage
2. When entering a chamber, the player transforms into a green frame
3. The green frame that was in the passage moves to fill the gap
4. The dots (black/white) seem to swap or transfer
Let me think about what the goal might be. The left chamber has a charcoal frame with a white center. The right chamber has a charcoal frame with a white center. 
The passage has a green frame with a white dot. When the player enters the right chamber, it becomes a green frame with a black dot.
Maybe the goal is to get the green frame with the WHITE dot into one of the chambers? Or maybe to match the colors?
Let me try moving LEFT again to see if the player can go into the left chamber.
Wait, actually let me re-examine. The player is now at [30,30]-[32,32] (passage). If I move LEFT, the player would go to the left chamber area.
Let me try that.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content: Now I see the mechanics more clearly. The player (id=7) is a white 3x3 square in the passage. When it moves into a chamber, it transforms into a green frame. The passage green frame shifts to fill the gap. Let me try moving LEFT into the left chamber to see what happens.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Try moving LEFT to enter the left chamber
result = action(['LEFT'])
print(f"Action result: {last_action_result}")

seg = current_frame.segmentation
print("\n=== Key nodes after LEFT ===")
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['id'] in [6, 7, 8, 9]:
        r_min = min(p[0] for p in n['boundary'])
        c_min = min(p[1] for p in n['boundary'])
        r_max = max(p[0] for p in n['boundary'])
        c_max = max(p[1] for p in n['boundary'])
        print(f"  id={n['id']:2d} color={n['color']} pixels={n['pixels']:3d} bounds=[{r_min},{c_min}]-[{r_max},{c_max}] children={n['children']}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
